skill.name: react-modernization
skill.description: Upgrade React applications, migrate class components to hooks, adopt React 18 features, and plan codemod-based refactors.
skill.summary: Modernize React applications with hooks, React 18 patterns, TypeScript guidance, and migration checklists.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high risk, but review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration. Most alerts are false positives from markdown code fences, React examples, and local API examples. The remaining risk is moderate because the playbook recommends npm, npx, and codemod commands that can install packages or rewrite project files.

medium_findings.title: Codemod and Package Commands Require User Oversight
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for command-execution risk, but not malicious behavior. The playbook recommends npm, npx, react-codeshift, codemod, and jscodeshift commands that can install packages or rewrite source files. These are legitimate React migration steps, but users should review package sources and run them in a clean branch.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain explicit package installation and codemod execution examples. The intent is legitimate React modernization, but these commands can change local files or execute third-party tooling.

low_findings.title: React Dynamic Imports Are Local Code-Splitting Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious script loading. The dynamic import examples load local React components for lazy routing and do not fetch remote code or construct module paths from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports are static relative paths inside a React.lazy example. No untrusted input, remote URL, or obfuscation is present.

low_findings.title: Fetch Calls Are Relative API Examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for network exfiltration. The fetch examples call relative application API paths during a data-fetching migration example and do not send secrets to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both calls use relative paths and appear in before-and-after React lifecycle examples. I found no evidence of credential collection or external transmission.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for Ruby or shell backtick execution. The cited lines are markdown code fences, inline path formatting, or JavaScript and JSX examples, not executable Ruby or shell interpolation inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows markdown formatting and tutorial code blocks. No skill file contains executable Ruby code or shell backtick substitution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text-Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptographic algorithms. The cited lines contain React migration prose, package commands, or codemod JavaScript, and I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak crypto usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines do not contain cryptographic APIs or algorithm names in context. The matches appear to be substring or markdown-related false positives.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Tutorial Structure
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for reconnaissance. The cited lines are React component lifecycle and hook examples, not commands that enumerate files, users, hosts, or system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside a React fetch lifecycle example and do not perform host or environment discovery. No evidence of system reconnaissance was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Codemod Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes npx and jscodeshift examples. This is useful for migration work, but users should review transforms before running them because codemods can execute package code and change many files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These lines explicitly recommend migration commands. The commands match the skill purpose, but they require user confirmation and source review.

content.value_statement: React upgrades often fail because lifecycle behavior, dependencies, and rendering changes are handled separately. This skill provides structured migration guidance for hooks, React 18 APIs, TypeScript, performance, and codemods.
actual_capabilities: Explains React 16 to 17 to 18 migration concerns.
Maps class component state and lifecycle methods to hooks.
Shows React 18 patterns such as createRoot, batching, transitions, and Suspense.
Provides codemod command examples for repetitive migrations.
Gives TypeScript conversion patterns for React components.
Includes a migration checklist for testing and rollout planning.
limitations: It does not run migrations or verify results by itself.
It does not replace project-specific regression testing.
Codemod examples require review before use in a real repository.
It focuses on React modernization, not full application architecture redesign.

use_cases.title: Upgrade a Legacy React App
use_cases.description: Plan a staged React upgrade and identify breaking changes before changing dependencies.

use_cases.title: Convert Class Components to Hooks
use_cases.description: Translate state, lifecycle methods, context usage, and data loading into functional patterns.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Migration Playbook
use_cases.description: Create a checklist for codemods, testing, performance checks, and rollout sequencing.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Upgrade Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a React application before dependency changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React project for upgrade readiness. Identify likely React 17 and React 18 issues, required dependency checks, and a safe migration order.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a Class Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this class component to a functional component with hooks. Preserve behavior, explain lifecycle mapping, and list tests I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Adopt React 18 Features
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate modernization after dependencies are updated.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React 18 code path. Recommend where createRoot, automatic batching, transitions, Suspense, or memoization should be used or avoided.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Codemod Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced migration across a large codebase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a codemod migration plan for this React repository. Separate automated transforms from manual review, include rollback steps, and define validation checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A team needs to move from React 16 class components to React 18.
output: A staged upgrade path from React 16 to 17 to 18.
A list of class component patterns that should be converted first.
Testing checkpoints for Strict Mode, effects, and rendering behavior.

output_examples.input: A component uses lifecycle methods for data fetching and cleanup.
output: A hook-based migration outline using state and effects.
Cleanup and dependency guidance for the effect.
A short verification plan for loading, updates, and unmount behavior.

output_examples.input: A large repository needs automated React refactoring.
output: A codemod plan with branch, backup, and review steps.
Guidance on which transforms are safer to automate.
A checklist for tests, linting, and manual inspection after each transform.

best_practices: Upgrade incrementally and keep each migration step reviewable.
Run tests and inspect Strict Mode warnings after each major change.
Review codemods before execution and apply them on a clean branch.
anti_patterns: Running codemods across the full repository without a rollback plan.
Replacing class components without preserving lifecycle cleanup behavior.
Adding memoization everywhere without measuring performance impact.

faq.question: Can this skill upgrade my repository automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and checklists. You must run commands and verify changes yourself.

faq.question: Does it support React 18 migration?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers createRoot, automatic batching, Strict Mode behavior, transitions, and Suspense patterns.

faq.question: Can it help migrate class components to hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains state, lifecycle, context, data fetching, cleanup, and custom hook patterns.

faq.question: Are the codemod examples safe to run?
faq.answer: They are standard migration examples, but they can change many files. Review tools first and use a clean branch.

faq.question: Does it cover TypeScript migration?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes basic component typing and generic component patterns for React.

faq.question: Is this useful for new React projects?
faq.answer: It is most useful for existing projects, but its React 18 and performance guidance can inform new work.
